coredata.dc:title: Reasonable design of supramolecular carbon dots with needle-like structures and regulation of tribological properties
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Pai
Wang, Chenchen
Lin, Hualin
Han, Sheng
Mao, Yuze
coredata.dc:description: The investigation of nanocrystal self-assembly into superlattices or superstructures stands as a crucial endeavor within the realm of nanoscience. Carbon dots, emerging as a novel class of nanomaterials, exhibit broad potential across multifaceted fields. However, despite their burgeoning applications, the exploration of their self-assembly behavior remains relatively sparse, particularly in the frictional phenomena. Herein, a unique acicular aggregate of carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) was synthesized through the reflux of long alkyl chains in chlorobenzene, with a distinctive needle-like structure. As a lubricant additive, the tribological test results presented that a notable decrease of 26.4 % in the coefficient of friction (COF) and 78.6 % in the wear scar diameter (WSD) of PAO. Furthermore, through the computational methodologies calculating the friction chemistry and lubrication state of the wear surface, the lubrication mechanism was revealed that formation of a thicker oil film between the friction pairs, thereby mitigating wear loss. This study provides a novel insight for the application of supramolecular carbonized polymer points in the friction-related applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Supramolecular carbonized polymer dots
Tribology
Lubrication
Wear

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Project Number 22075183, 22278269 and 21975161), Industrial Collaborative Innovation Project of Shanghai (Project Number 2021-cyxt1-kj37 and XTCX-KJ-2022-70), Research and Innovation Project of Shanghai Municipal Education Commission (Project Number 2023ZKZD54),Shanghai \"Science and Technology Innovation Action Plan\" Morning Star Cultivation (Sailing Program 22YF1447500) and \u201CChen Guang\u201D project (22CGA75) supported by Shanghai Municipal Education Commission and Shanghai Education Development Foundation and Collaborative Innovation Center of Fragrance Flavour and Cosmetics.

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Project Number 22075183, 22278269 and 21975161), Industrial Collaborative Innovation Project of Shanghai (Project Number 2021-cyxt1-kj37 and XTCX-KJ-2022-70), Research and Innovation Project of Shanghai Municipal Education Commission (Project Number 2023ZKZD54),Shanghai \u201CScience and Technology Innovation Action Plan\u201D Morning Star Cultivation (Sailing Program 22YF1447500) and \u201CChen Guang\u201D project (22CGA75) supported by Shanghai Municipal Education Commission and Shanghai Education Development Foundation and Collaborative Innovation Center of Fragrance Flavour and Cosmetics.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image and corresponding size distribution of CPDMB, CPDCB and CPDBB (a, d and g) and AFM images of CPDMB, CPDCB and CPDBB(b, e and h), POM images of CPDMB、CPDCB and CPDBB (c, f and i).

(a) FT-IR of the CPDs and starting material (C14–NH2), (b) TGA traces of CPDs, (c) Typical powder X-ray diffraction spectra of the CPDs, (d) DSC traces from the second heating–cooling circles of CPDMB, (e) DSC traces from the second heating–cooling circles of CPDCB, (f) DSC traces from the second heating–cooling circles of CPDBB, (g) The interrelationship between viscosity and shear rate for 0.07 wt% CPDs in PAO viscous state at 20 °C, (h) Changes in viscosity for 0.07 wt% CPDs in PAO state with the increase of shear time when shear rate was 1500 s−1, (f) Effect of temperature on the viscosity for 0.07 wt% CPDs in PAO state when shear rate is 1500 s−1.

(a, e, i) The XPS full survey of CPDMB, CPDCB and CPDBB; (b, c, d)C1s, N 1s and O1s XPS spectra of CPDMB; (f, g, h) C1s, N1s and O1s XPS spectra of CPDCB and (j, k, l) C1s, N1s and O1s XPS spectra of CPDBB.

Coefficient of friction(a)and wear scar diameter(b) of balls lubricated by CPDCB-PAO under 294 N, 1200r. (c) Coefficient of friction curves of 0.7 wt% CPDCB with different load (1200r, 25 °C) and (d) 0.7 wt% CPDCB average coefficient of friction and wear volume under different load (1200r, 25 °C). (e) Coefficient of friction curves of 0.7 wt% CPDCB with different rate (294 N, 25 °C) and (f) 0.7 wt% CPDCB average coefficient of friction and wear volume under different rate (294 N, 25 °C). Coefficient of friction (g) and wear scar diameter (h) of balls lubricated by 0.7 wt% CPDMB, CPDCB and CPDBB in PAO under 294 N, 1200r for 7200s.
The 3D topographies and corresponding cross-sectional profiles and SEM of the worn surfaces with PAO (a, e, i, m), CPDMB (b, f, j, n), CPDCB (c, g, k, o) and CPDBB (d, h, l, p) in PAO at 294 N.
XPS spectra of wear track on the lower ball lubricated with PAO (a–d), CPDMB-PAO (e–h), CPDCB-PAO (i–l) and CPDBB-PAO (m–p).
Raman spectra of the wear scars lubricated by the (a) (b)PAO 6 blended with the CPDCB additive, (c) pure PAO lube base oil.

Cross-sectional SEM image from FIB cutting worn surface lubricated with CPDCB in PAO 6 base oil (0.07 wt%, 1200 rpm, 30 min, 294 N) in low (a) and high (b) magnification. Element distribution of tribofilm lubricated with 0.07 wt% CPDCB. (c–g) EDS mapping results for the tribofilm. (h) Element content of the worn surface lubricated from CPDCB-PAO.

The Hmin and
λ
of CPDs.

simple-head.ce:title: Reasonable design of supramolecular carbon dots with needle-like structures and regulation of tribological properties

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanghai Aerospace Control Technology Institute, Shanghai, 201100, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The investigation of nanocrystal self-assembly into superlattices or superstructures stands as a crucial endeavor within the realm of nanoscience. Carbon dots, emerging as a novel class of nanomaterials, exhibit broad potential across multifaceted fields. However, despite their burgeoning applications, the exploration of their self-assembly behavior remains relatively sparse, particularly in the frictional phenomena. Herein, a unique acicular aggregate of carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) was synthesized through the reflux of long alkyl chains in chlorobenzene, with a distinctive needle-like structure. As a lubricant additive, the tribological test results presented that a notable decrease of 26.4 % in the coefficient of friction (COF) and 78.6 % in the wear scar diameter (WSD) of PAO. Furthermore, through the computational methodologies calculating the friction chemistry and lubrication state of the wear surface, the lubrication mechanism was revealed that formation of a thicker oil film between the friction pairs, thereby mitigating wear loss. This study provides a novel insight for the application of supramolecular carbonized polymer points in the friction-related applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The intertwined issues of energy efficiency and environmental preservation represent significant challenges constraining global development efforts. Governments worldwide are confronted with the imperative task of enhancing energy utilization efficiency while simultaneously addressing and mitigating environmental pollution [1]. Notably, friction and wear phenomena pervade various aspects of daily life, engendering mechanical failures and energy dissipation, which account for a substantial fraction of global energy consumption and economic losses [2]. To alleviate these adverse effects, the application of lubricants represents a primary strategy, aiming to diminish solid-solid contact between rolling or sliding surfaces, thereby transitioning from a dry friction state to a lubricated state and improving energy conversion efficiency. Lubricants consist of a multi-component mixture of comprising predominantly base oils, constituting approximately 90 %, complemented by additives, which account for approximately 10 % [3]. Many researchers have developed new additives to replace traditional conventional lubricant additives containing elements such as phosphorus, sulfur, and chlorine. The advent of nanotechnology has notably catalyzed the emergence of nanomaterial-based lubrication systems, wherein the incorporation of minute quantities of nanoparticles (NPs) has demonstrated favorable outcomes in terms of friction and wear reduction. This efficacy is attributable to the diminutive size of nano-additives, facilitating their entry into contact areas and consequent positive lubrication effects [4]. However, when they are used as lubricating oil additives, they tend to agglomerate and precipitate. Usually, the adsorption, reaction and coating of the modifier on the surface of the nanoparticle are used to form the micelle structure with the nanoparticle as the colloidal core and the outer end as the oilophilic group to improve the dispersion stability [5].Addressing these limitations is essential to unlocking the full potential and technical viability of nano-lubricants, thereby advancing their developmental trajectory and practical utility (see Table 1 ).

Carbon dots represent quasi-zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, characterized by dimensions less than 10 nm in all directions [6]. A large number of literature records the frictional properties of carbon dots synthesized by various methods such as laser etching [7], pyrolysis [8], electrochemical etching [9]in aqueous solution [10]and non-aqueous solvent [11]. Cai [12]et al. utilize boron-containing ionic liquid and glutamic acid precursorsto synthesize boron nitrogen co doped carbon dots as polyethylene glycol (PEG) additives through pyrolysis, significantly enhancing anti-wear and anti-friction properties of PEG. Mou [13]et al. synthesized a series of zwitterionic polyimide grafted carbon dots, and reported that the carbon dots were compressed through hydration, adsorption, and deposition mechanisms to form a friction film with a thickness of about 320 nm, effectively reducing fatigue damage on the friction steel surface. However, the exploration of carbon dots in supramolecular self-assembly has been relatively overlooked. Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) constituted a subclass of carbon dots proposed featuring a carbonized core enveloped by a polymer shell [14]. In fact, most CDs synthesized from molecules or polymers using the "bottom-up" approach were CPDS, where dehydration crosslinking controls their performance, depending on the delicate balance between polymerization and carbonization processes [15]. While multifunctional organic compounds were conventionally employed as raw materials for CPDs synthesis, the utilization of monofunctional compounds, particularly aliphatic species, has been relatively unexplored. Notably, aliphatic compounds, abundant and cost-effective, offered promising avenues for CPDs preparation. Compared with traditional carbon dots, carbonized polymer dots exhibited unique core-shell structure and polymer characteristics. At present, several scholars revealed that CPDs have found application in various domains such as photosensitivity therapy [16], photoelectric detection [17], and analytical assays [18]. While their potential in lubrication has not been fully explored in the literature.

In this study, CPDs with single substituted aliphatic compounds as the sole carbon source and surface modified with alkyl groups were synthesized by reflux in three solvents with different degrees of polarization. The three solvents facilitated the formation of different micro lotion environments and actively participate in the formation process of CPDs, thus affecting the structure and performance of the three kinds of CPDs. The synthesized CPDs exhibited favorable solubility in conventional organic solvents. Moreover, comprehensive analyses were conducted to elucidate the composition and internal structure of the CPDs, followed by a detailed investigation of their tribological properties in PAO6 oil (the best tolerance for additives) solvents. The findings revealed that CPDs exhibited a heterogeneous structure, characterized by the carbon core being enveloped by long alkyl chains on the surface, which tightly wrap to form needle shaped aggregates. Remarkably, the CPDCB demonstrated the best lubrication performance. The underlying friction mechanism was probed through a combination of chemical analysis of the frictional surface and computational assessment of the lubrication state. This study carved out a novel pathway for the development of CPDs in the field of supramolecular self-assembly, particularly in the context of frictional systems.

Myristylamine (96 %+, RG), toluene (99 %, RG) chlorobenzene (99 %, RG), bromobenzene (99 %, RG) were supplied by Shanghai Titan Technology Co., ltd (China). Ethanol and dichloromethane were obtained from local supplier with the quality of analytical grade. Commercial aggregation α Olefin (PAO 6) was used as the base oil supplied by Beijing Xirunte Trading Co., Ltd. In addition, the used steel balls (Diameter φ 12.7 mm, HRC 59–61) were made of GCr15.

In order to obtain a carbon point with good oil solubility, the polymer chains on the surface are retained and a highly cross-linked polymer cluster is formed by randomly dewatering myristylamine by "bottom-up" method. The synthesis of carbonized polymer dots was accomplished via a reflux heating method employing myristylamine as the sole carbon source. Specifically, 0.1 mol of myristylamine was dissolved in 100 mL of toluene, followed by gradual heating to 50 °C under magnetic stirring for 30 minutes. Subsequently, the temperature was incrementally raised to 110 °C, and reflux was maintained for 48 hours upon reaching thermal stability. Upon cessation of the reaction, the mixture was allowed to naturally cool to room temperature, followed by solvent removal via rotary evaporation. Thin layer chromatography (TLC) was employed for identifying a suitable solvent system for subsequent silica gel chromatography purification. Ethanol was selected as the eluent, facilitating the facile separation of the desired fluorescent CPDs from the raw materials. Additionally, the purification process on the column was monitored using ultraviolet lamps. The eluent was subjected to vacuum distillation to eliminate residual solvent, and the resulting product was dried under vacuum at 40 °C for three days. The resulting product was named as CPDMB. The solvent was replaced with chlorobenzene and bromobenzene respectively, the synthesis route is consistent with the synthesis method of CPDMB, and the obtained product was named CPDCB and CPDBB.

Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Nicolet-6700 FT-IR spectrophotometer (Thermo Instruments Inc.,USA) with KBr pellets. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on an ESCALAB 250 Xi system (Thermo Instruments Inc.USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM)was performed using a JEM 210OF transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). Nano Series Dynamic Light Scattering Experiment (DLS, Malvern Instruments Co.). Atomic force microscopic (AFM) images were recorded on a Dimension lcon ScanAsyst atomic force microscope in air mode (Bruker，Germany). X-Ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was carried out using X-ray powder diffractometer system (D8ADVANCE). Rheology tested using Discovery HR-2TA instrument. The thermogravimetric analysis was conducted by DSC 822e in a nitrogen atmosphere at a rate of 10 °C/min. Differential scanning calorimetry was tested with DSC-Q2000 instrument produced by TA Company. The samples were tested in an alumina crucible under nitrogen atmosphere at a variable temperature rate of 10 °C/min. The wear volume and morphology of the lower ball were obtained via a NPFLEX three-dimensional (3D) surface profiler by Bruker (USA). The morphologies of the worn surface were inspected through JSM-6610 LV scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Japan).

The tribological properties of CPDMB, CPDCB and CPDBB in PAO 6 base oil were tested with MMW-1 four-ball testing machine at 30 min, 1200 rpm and room temperature. The mean coefficient of friction (ACOF) and the mean wear scar diameter (MWSD) were measured through the four-ball wear test, providing an intuitive assessment of the wear resistance conferred by CPDMB, CPDCB, and CPDBB additives. In these tests, the typical AISI52100 steel balls (12.7 mm in diameter, hardness of about 59–61 HRC) were employed, with one upper ball subjected to rotation against three stationary steel balls clamped within the holder. All friction tests were performed at room temperature (23 ± 2 °C). Before friction test, balls and plates were placed in ethanol solution for ultrasonic cleaning for 30 min. Each friction test was iterated three times under identical conditions to ensure the reproducibility and reliability of the results. Three-dimensional topography of wear tracks was measured by Bruker Contour GT (Germany) surface mapping microscope profilometer. Additionally, the morphology and chemical composition of wear scars were assessed via scanning electron microscopy (SEM), energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) techniques, facilitating a comprehensive understanding of the wear mechanisms and material interactions underlying the observed frictional behavior. Raman spectra of the worn surfaces under the lubricants were collected using a Horiba LabRam HR800, spectrometer equipped with excitation argonion lasers working at 532 nm. The wear tracks were crosssectioned using a focused ion beam scanning electron microscope (FIB-SEM) (Scios 2 HiVac, FEI, Netherlands), and a protective Pt coating was sputtered onto the wear surface before slicing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical and structure characterization of the CPDs
ce:section.ce:para: CPDMB, CPDCB and CPDBB were prepared from the long-chain fatty amine myristylamine as the only carbon source by bottom-up reflux in different solvents (toluene, chlorobenzene and bromobenzene). The morphology of CPDMB, CPDCB and CPDBB appeared mainly spheroidal and evenly distributed, with average particle sizes of 1.25 nm, 1.73 nm and 2.25 nm, respectively (Fig. 1 a, d and 1g). It has been observed that solvent properties during pyrolysis are closely linked to their polarizability. Among the three solvents, the polarizability (α) had the following sequence: toluene < chlorobenzene < bromobenzene. Consequently, the increase in particle size consistent with the increase carbonization degree of tetradecylamine across the solvent series. In essence, higher polarizability (α) fostered the pyrolysis of tetradecylamine[19]. The morphology of CPDMB, CPDCB, and CPDBB is observed to be very similar to polymer clusters. Dynamic light scattering (Fig. S1) revealed average particle sizes of 75 nm, 651 nm, and 254 nm for CPDMB, CPDCB and CPDBB. Notably, CPDCB exhibited the largest hydrodynamic diameter among the three CPDs, indicative of a core-shell structure and abundant alkyl chains on their surfaces across all three CPDs variants [20].

AFM analysis showed that the average height values of CPDMB, CPDCB and CPDBB were approximately 1.8 nm, 2.2 nm and 2.8 nm respectively. Notably, the corresponding diameter values obtained from AFM were notably much larger than the height measurements, with CPDCB exhibiting the maximum diameter.(Fig. 1b, e, and 1h). This disparity in size indicated the presence of a core-shell structure in CPDMB, CPDCB, and CPDBB, consisting of a central carbon core and surface polymer chains [21]. Typically, TEM recorded the size of the carbon core of carbonized polymer dots due to the easy degradation of polymer segments under strong electric fields and the limited contrast of the polymer chain structure. Therefore, observing the polymer structure inside the shell presented inherent challenges [22].

To further elucidate the structural characteristics of CPDMB, CPDCB, and CPDBB, heating and cooling cycles were performed on the three types of CPDs, and solid precipitation was observed gradually during cooling. POM was subsequently performed at the cooling temperature (Fig. 1c, 1f, and 1i). Notably, CPDMB exhibited a fine-grained texture at 45 °C, while CPDBB showcased larger particles at 40 °C. In contrast, during the cooling process, CPDCB continuously presents fine grain and needle-like structure, and we infer this conformation is caused by the entanglement of polymer chains (Fig. 1f and S2). These observations provided valuable insights into the structural morphology and thermal behavior of CPDMB, CPDCB, and CPDBB. At the same time, the dispersion stability of CPDs as NPs is important to verify its stability in oil. By comparing the UV spectra of CPDs-PAO before and after one week, the peak value did not shift or decrease significantly, indicating that CPDs can remain stably dispersed in the PAO for a long time without significant sedimentation (Fig. S3).

In order to obtain more structural details, the CPDMB, CPDCB and CPDBB were subjected to Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) and Fourier Transform Infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) measurements. From 1H NMR (Fig. S4a), the presence of sp3 hybridized carbon in CPDs was detected by 1H NMR spectroscopy (Fig. S4a), mainly within the range of 1.60–0.85 ppm, which is due to the presence of electron withdrawing groups such as benzene rings reduces electron density and thus reduces the shielding effect on the magnetic field. 13C NMR (Fig. S4b) consistented with the results of 1H NMR spectroscopy. From FT-IR (Fig. 2 a), three CPDs have similar FT-IR spectra, indicating similar chemical binding states. Compared with the myristylamine, new signals appeared in the infrared spectra of CPDs. The absorption at 1576-1559 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of the CC in the benzene ring skeleton [23], indicative of presence of obvious conjugated structures within the carbon dots. The absorption at 1336-1384 cm−1 and 1159 cm−1 corresponds to the vibrations of C–N and C–O [24], indicative of carbon chain breakage, molecular condensation, and organic cyclization occurred during the formation of carbon dots. The medium band at 723 cm−1 in myristylamine can be attributed to the shear vibration of methylidene in an alkyl chain with no less than 4 carbon atoms [25]. This band still exists in the same position at CPDs, indicating the retention of alkyl chains during pyrolysis.

As shown in Fig. 2b,there were two obvious diffraction peaks in the XRD pattern, located at about 6.2° and 23° respectively. According to previous reports [26], it can be observed that the X-ray diffraction pattern of carbon point-like materials often shows a wide dispersion peak, which appeared in the range of 2θ = 21–25°. We speculate that this is caused by the relatively regular arrangement of similar carbon skeletons produced by cross-linking or carbonization. In addition, the appearance of the diffraction peak at 6.2° represents an increase in the interplanar spacing within the carbide polymer point particles, with the peak of CPDCB being most pronounced at 6.2°, indicative of average distance between the crosslinked polymer skeletons within CPDCB particles is the highest. These results indicated that the internal structure of carbonized polymer dots is filled with cross-linked polymer networks, further confirming the that CPDs was composed of a large number of disordered polymer chains, resulting in strong XRD signals of the amorphous polymer carbon skeleton on the surface.

The thermotropic properties of the CPDs were probed by TGA (Fig. 2c) and DSC (Fig. 2d, e and 2f) measurements. Similar to classical polymers such as polyethylene, polystyrene, and polymethacrylate [27], CPDs also exhibit a typical thermal decomposition process, indicating the presence of polymer chains in CPDs. Some mass loss was observed below 100 °C, due to the physical adsorption of moisture in the air, which also confirmed the strong affinity of N atoms in the polymer for water [28]. For CPDMB and CPDBB, the DSC traces of the two samples have the same characteristics, showing a pair of small peaks before the clearance point. The clear points of the two samples are 62.9 °C and 53.9 °C respectively at temperature rise, and 51.2 °C and 47.2 °C at temperature drop. For CPDMB, before the clear bright spot, a relatively wide region appears between 35.5 °C and 50.6 °C during the heating process, and between 32.4 °C and 39.2 °C during the cooling process. This less obvious chowder phenomenon is more common in nanomaterials with polydisperse size and shape [29].

The viscosity of PAO is less affected by the environment, resulting in high viscosity at high shear and high temperature. When the viscosity of the lubricant is too high, it will hinder the lubrication effect of the lubricant. At constant temperature (20 °C), as with typical linear polymers, the viscosity of CPDs-PAO gradually decreases and stabilizes as the shear rate increases, showing shear thinning properties (Fig. 2g). When the temperature (20 °C) and shear rate (1500/r) remain unchanged, the viscosity of CPDs-PAO hardly changes with the extension of shear time, indicating the high stability of this system [30](Fig. 2h). At the same time, with the increase of temperature, the interaction between CPDs polymer chains is weakened, the distance between particles becomes larger, the attraction decreases, and the viscosity gradually decreases(Fig. 2i). The viscosity of CPDCB-PAO is the highest in CPDs-PAO, which confirms that the polymer chain on the surface of CPDCB is longer, consistent with the conclusions of AFM and DLS.

The XPS full spectrum of CPDMB、CPDCB and CPDBB were showed in Fig. 3 a, e and 3i, respectively. Predominantly, carbon(C) constitutes the main element in CPDMB, CPDCB and CPDBB, accompanied by minor quantities of nitrogen(N) and oxygen(O). The C1s spectrum showed three different types of carbon, including graphite or aliphatic carbon (CC and C–C), carbon nitride (C–N/C–O) and carbon oxide (O–CO) at 284.6 eV, 286.0 eV and 288.6 eV, respectively (Fig. 3b, f and 3j) [31]. N1s analysis revealed the co-presence of two different nitrogen types, pyrrole nitrogen at 399.8 eV and graphite nitrogen at 401.65 eV (Fig. 3c, g and 3k) [32]. Since all three CPDs are synthesized from the same raw material, the above three CPDs have similar C1s and N1s. However, changes in the reaction process produce different proportions of carbon and nitrogen in the final carbonized polymer dots. Specifically, CPDCB predominantly featured pyrrole nitrogen, indicative of strong and stable conjugation ability, whereas the other two variants exhibit pyridine nitrogen dominance, characterized by heightened reactivity.

The above analysis results provided insights into the chemical composition and functional groups present in CPDs, and elucidated their thermal stability. This multi-faceted characterization approach implied that CPDs possessed a core-shell-like structure and polymer properties, with highlighting the enhanced polymer chain abundance and aggregation propensity of CPDCB.

Fig. 4 showed the effect of PAO and the CPDCB-PAO on the friction coefficient curves and wear diameters in different conditions. Obviously, the lubrication performance of PAO is worse than that of CPDs-PAO, and its friction coefficient and wear rate are higher than that of CPDs, which is due to the inability to form a continuous lubricating film in time to reduce friction and conform to boundary lubrication. Compared with other CDs-based lubricating oils, the advantages of CPDs-PAO are not significant, but the amount of additives(CDs) used is greatly reduced. In other words, when the amount of CPDs added is 1/2 or even 1/5 of other jobs of additives, a considerable lubricating effect can be achieved. Meanwhile, comparing the lubrication performance of three CPDs-PAOs is also meaningful. The COF of CPDCB-PAO is consistently lower than the other two CPDs base oils (Fig. S6 and Fig. S7). Specifically, the average friction coefficient and wear volume of CPDCB-PAO were 0.064 and 6.28 × 103 μm3, representing reductions of 26.4 % and 78.6 % compared to pure PAO (0.087 and 2.94 × 104 μm3). The COF and WSD of CPDMB-PAO were respectively reduced to 22.9 % and 62.43 % than that of PAO (Fig. S6). Moreover, compared to pure PAO, the CPDBB-PAO exhibited a reduction of COF and WSD with 20.7 % and 52.87 % (Fig. S7). As shown in Fig. 4a and 4b, the lubricity of CPDCB-PAO initially decreased slightly and then stabilized with the increasing time. With the increase of CPDCB concentration, the COF was first decreased and then slightly increased, and the lubrication effect was the best when the addition amount was 0.7 wt%. When the amount of CPDCB was less than 0.7 wt%, the friction coefficient curve was higher, likely due to the low thickness of the friction film and the discontinuity stability caused by insufficient CPDCB, and the surface adhesion caused by the convex and convex contact of the friction pair [33]. when CPDCB concentration exceeded 0.7 wt%, the aggregation of CPDCB particles exacerbated tribological properties by acting as wear particles [34]. The COF and WSD under different load and speed of CPDCB-PAO were also investigated (Fig. 4c–f). The friction test results showed that 0.7 wt% CPDCB has the best tribological performance at 294 N, 1200 r. The chemical stability of lubricating additives is very important, so the lubrication performance and stability of special media such as acid (5 % H2SO4) and alkali (5 % CaCO3) were tested (Fig. S5), which showed compared with acidified PAO and alkalized PAO, CPDCB-PAO showed lower COF and smaller WSD. Meanwhile, the COF and WSD under long-term lubrication of CPDs-PAO were investigated (Fig. 4g and h), CPDCB-PAO exhibited the best stable lubrication.

Further observations were conducted to assess wear conditions, focusing on the three-dimensional morphology, corresponding section curve, and SEM images of the wear surface (Fig. 5 ). Notably, the wear rate of the lower steel ball after friction was visually discerned as follows: CPDCB-PAO < CPDBB-PAO < CPDMB-PAO < PAO. As shown in Fig. 5a–e, the three-dimensional morphology of steel balls treated with PAO lubricant showed large wear area and deep wear depths with a maximum depth of 3 μm. Severe adhesive wear was evident, characterized by extensive tearing and peeling of the metal surface (Fig. 5m). After adding CPDMB-PAO, the adhesive wear of the surface was significantly reduced, with only a few metal pieces shed from the friction pair surface (Fig. 5n). This phenomenon mirrored the morphology and structure of wear products reported by previous studies, highlighting needle-like wear products with sharp micro-convex structures [35,36]. In the case of nano CPDCB as lubricant, the three-dimensional shape of the sphere had almost no serious wear area, with no accumulation of debris observed at the edges of wear marks, indicative of superior wear resistance. Additionally, for the CPDMB-PAO treated steel balls, the wear marks in the middle portion were notably smaller compared to the wear edge. This disparity can be attributed to the middle portion as the initial contact point between the two balls, leading to the appearance of wear pear grooves. Furthermore, the continuous accumulation of CPDBB additive contributed to wear middle part height exceeding that of the wear edge in the cross-section diagram (Fig. 5f–h).

During the friction process, a myriad of intricate physical and chemical interactions take place between the lubricant and the metal surface, constituting the crux for enhancing its anti-wear and anti-friction properties. In order to explore the surface chemical friction film on the different wear surfaces, the chemical composition on the wear surface were analyzed. The XPS results shown in Fig. 6 presented that comparison of the C1s spectra across the four cases revealed the presence of characteristic peaks corresponding to C–C/CC (284.7 eV), C–OH (286.8 eV), and O–CO (288.6 eV), originating from organic matter and CPDs in the lubricant [37]. The change of the relative content of each group indicated that the nanoparticles and organic molecules were involved in the continuous fracture and formation of covalent bonds in Fig. 6(f–j, n) [38,39], the appearance of N–O and N–H bond characteristic peaks corresponding to 402 eV and 396 eV in the N1s spectrum showed that after adding the above three carbon points lubricants, the N group transformed into –NO2 and N–H···Π weak interactions were formed during the friction process, increasing the strength of the friction film [40]. Moreover, the proportion of N–O and N–H bond characteristic peaks in CPDCB is higher, which was consistent with the actual friction experiment result(Fig. 6j). Fe2p3/2 peaks ranged in the 709–715 eV and Fe2p1/2 peaks ranged in the 722–729 eV from the Fe2p spectrum denoted the presence of iron oxides such as Fe3O4 and Fe2O3 [41]. At the same time, the presence of 720 eV characteristic peak in Fig. 6 (l, p) indicated that the proportions of Fe2+ in Fe elements after friction of CPDCB and CPDBB were higher than that in CPDMB and base oil, suggesting a lower Fe oxidation and bolstering the protective effect of the friction film during friction. These analyses manifested the formation of the chemisorption tribofilm rather than the physisorption of the PAO. Moreover, CPDs-PAO reacted with tribo-pairs to produce tribo-chemical film, further protecting the surface against undesirable wear and friction. The synergistic effects contributed to the remarkable friction-reducing and anti-wear functions.

Further verification of the frictional reaction of CPDs as additive in PAO was conducted, and the composition of the worn surface of CPDCB-PAO was determined using Raman spectroscopy(Fig. 7 ). Fig. 7 (a, b) shows the Raman spectra of three random locations on the wear scar under CPDCB-PAO lubrication. According to the literature, the Raman peaks at 290, 406, 599, 610 and 659 cm−1 were attributed to the formation of Fe2O3 [42–44], and the peaks at 670 cm−1 was due to formation of Fe3O4 [44]. Consequently, under CPDCB-PAO lubrication, the Raman peaks observed at 292.9, 401.6, 408.3, 419.6, 596.2, 598.4, 617.5, 648.2, 654.7 and 667.6 cm−1 indicate the formation of Fe2O3 [45]. Moreover, peaks at 883.2, 868.4, 899.8 and 920.5 cm−1 were associated with C–C–O or C–C–C [46,47]. And two characteristic peaks around 1350 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1, namely the D and G bands of carbon dots, indicated that CPDCB with added PAO was transferred to the corresponding spheres during friction, forming a transfer film.

To observe the tribofilm more intuitively, we randomly cut the wear trajectory of the steel ball surface lubricated with CPDCB-PAO by using a focused ion beam (FIB) (Fig. 8 , Supporting Information). EDS in Fig. 8c-g showed a clear boundary between Pt and Fe substrate, indicative of the formation of a friction film on the metal surface with a thickness of about 300 nm(Fig. 8b). The composition of the friction film was mainly composed of C, N, O and so on decomposed from the combination of organic CPDCB and PAO. Among them, the content of C element was relatively high, which may be due to the accumulation of a large amount of CPDCB in the wear zone (Fig. 8c and 8g).

To speculate on the potential mechanism behind achieving lubricity of CPDs in PAO was proposed and discussed. In this study, the steel surface has periodic wear and tear, so it is not suitable to measure the film thickness by optical interferometry. The four-ball friction and wear experiment belong to the point contact type. According to the Hamrock-Dowson [48] elliptical contact minimum film thickness (Hmin) formula:
(1)
H min
= 3.63
R x
U ‾
0.68
G 0.49
W ‾
0.073
(
1 − 0.61
e
− 0.68 k
)

The detailed calculation process for these parameters can be found in the Supporting Information. Additionally, the lubrication state was classified by utilizing the ratio of the film thickness to the equivalent roughness (
λ
) in the following equation:
(2)
λ =
h min
σ 1 2
+
σ 2 2
where
σ 1
and
σ 2
(more details are available in the Supporting Information) are the average surface roughness of the wear trajectories of the top ball and the three bottom balls. As indicated in formula 2, the lambda factor is affected by the minimum film thickness and surface roughness. According to the existing literature [49], the EHL interval is λ ≥ 3.0, and the mixed lubrication interval is 0.5 < λ < 3.0, and the boundary lubrication interval is λ ≤ 0.5. This indicates that during the stage, the lubrication regime of the CPDCB-PAO lubricant is in mixed lubrication, CPDMB-PAO and CPDBB-PAO lubricant are in boundary lubrication.

Based on the above experimental and calculation results, the possible lubrication mechanism of CPDs in PAO was proposed and discussed (Fig. 9 ). The contact interface of the two friction pairs presented two lubrication states, namely boundary lubrication (CPDMB-PAO and CPDBB-PAO) and mixed lubrication (CPDCB-PAO). When using CPDMB-PAO or CPDBB-PAO as lubricants, fine particles (CPDMB and CPDBB) were uniformly dispersed in the PAO and move rapidly and at high speed between metal surfaces. These nanoparticles have strong adsorbability and are preferentially adsorbed on the surface of steel balls, forming nano film. By utilizing the high kinetic energy of fluids, the roughness of the friction surface can be reduced, thus improving the surface quality through the polishing effect [50]. Additionally, the generated heat promotes the gradual formation of a composite frictional chemical layer through the frictional chemical reaction between the lubricant and the steel surface, proving to be effective boundary lubricants. When CPDCB-PAO was used as a lubricant, the CPDCB undergoed adsorption and polishing during the initial friction process, slightly reducing the friction coefficient at the onset of friction (Fig. 4c). After that, CPDCB accumulated continuously on the contact zone. Under the action of pressure and shear, the needle-like lamellar aggregation in CPDCB-PAO exhibited interlayer sliding effect, where the friction acting on the metal surface is partially relieved by the internal friction of the nanoneedles. Moreover, nanoneedles might disintegrate into fine particles under pressure, enabling these fine nanocrystals to roll between friction surfaces akin to ball bearings, thereby converting sliding friction into rolling friction [51,52]. The previous studies [51] have shown that the combined use of layered and spherical nanoparticles exhibited synergistic lubrication properties effectively reducing friction and wear. Similarly, considering the motion mode of the nanoparticles, the rolling motion of these fine nanocrystals further enhanced the interlayer sliding effect of the aggregate nanoneedles. Moreover, from their physical and chemical properties, these nanocrystals can segregate different aggregates and attenuate aggregation and entanglement, thereby inhibiting the interlayer slip effect.

At the same time, in order to investigate the differences between CPDs-PAO friction films from another aspect, Fig. S11 showed fluorescence emission spectrum of CPDMB-PAO (a), CPDCB-PAO (b) and CPDBB-PAO (c) before and after friction. The luminescence mechanism of CPDs is complex [53]. In this study, due to the different solvents used in the synthesis, different luminescence intensities were observed for the three CPDs [54]. Remarkably, the fluorescence intensity of CPDCB-PAO exhibited the most significant decrease both before and after friction. This decline suggested a weakened radiative transition and enhanced non-radiative attenuation [55], implying energy release in the form of heat during the friction process. This played a positive role in the formation of friction film.

This work explored the synthesis of needle-like carbonized polymer dots aggregates with excellent oil solubility through the reflux of monofunctional aliphatic compound in three polarizing solvents. Detailed examinations of the composition and internal structure of these aggregates were conducted, alongside discussions on their tribological properties in PAO. The results of tribological behaviour revealed a notable decrease of 26.4 % in the COF and 78.6 % in the WSD of PAO when supplemented with 0.7 wt% CPDCB. Through the investigation of the wear surface and the calculation of the mathematical model, it is concluded that the unique lubrication effect of CPDCB is due to the large acicular supramolecular adsorption between the friction pairs to form a thicker oil film, and at the same time, part of the friction acting on the metal surface is alleviated by the internal friction of the acicular carbonized polymer aggregate, thus reducing the wear loss. This study underscores the potential of utilizing carbonized polymer dots for self-assembly behavior, offering valuable insights into their application in lubrication systems.

Pai Yu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Software, Formal analysis, Data curation. Chenchen Wang: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Conceptualization. Hualin Lin: Project administration, Investigation. Sheng Han: Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition. Yuze Mao: Supervision.
Any additional relevant notes should be placed here.
